---
title: "Can a DEM alone find the pour point?"
description: "Comparing two ways to locate where a watershed drains, using nothing but a 10-meter elevation model."
date: 2026-05-01
categories: [hydrology, Python, GIS]
---

::: {.callout-note}
Poster, Cal Poly CAFES Spring Student Research Symposium, May 2026. Faculty
co-author Dr. Aakash Ahamed and student co-author Dillen Corlett. I built the
analysis; the collaboration set the research question.
:::

## The question

A watershed's pour point is the single location where all surface water in the
basin exits. Finding it usually takes field knowledge or a known gauge. This
project asks whether a bare 10-meter digital elevation model, with no other
data, is enough to locate it, and how much the answer depends on the method you
choose.

## Approach

Two methods run side by side on the same watershed and the same elevation model:

- Geomorphon-based terrain classification
- Conventional D8 flow-accumulation

Both are implemented in Python. The comparison looks at where each method places
the outlet and how far the two disagree, with a discussion of the uncertainty
that disagreement implies.

## Tools

- 10-meter digital elevation model as the only input
- Python, with WhiteboxTools and pysheds

<!-- [VERIFY] Two titles appear in vault records: "Can a DEM Alone Find the Pour
Point?" and "Automated Pour Point Detection from 10m DEMs." Confirm the poster's
final title before publishing. -->
